---
title: "Tidy data and data files"
output:
  beamer_presentation:
    theme: "default"
    colortheme: "default"
    fonttheme: "default"
  ioslides_presentation: default
  slidy_presentation: default
header-includes:
- \usepackage{hyperref}
- \usepackage{caption}
- \usepackage[normalem]{ulem}
- \definecolor{links}{HTML}{2A1B81}
- \hypersetup{colorlinks,linkcolor=,urlcolor=links}
colorlinks: true
linkcolor: blue
urlcolor: blue
---

```{r, echo = FALSE}
library(knitr);
opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE);
```




## Background mathematics

\Huge

- Addition
- Subtraction
- Multiplication
- Division
- Exponents
- Roots

## Addition and Subtraction

\Huge

**Addition**

- Natural numbers
- 1, 2, 3, 4, ... \pause\vspace{2mm}

**Subtraction**

- Integers
- ..., -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, ...


## Inequalities

\Huge

**Greater than (>)**

$$6 > 5$$ \pause\vspace{4mm}

**Less than (<)**

$$-4 < 2$$


## Inequalities

\LARGE

**Greater than or equal to ($\geq$)**

$$P \geq 0.1$$ 

\pause\vspace{4mm}

**Less than or equal to ($\leq$)**

$$P \leq 0.05$$

## Multiplication and Division

\LARGE

**Multiplication**

- Add a number multiple times
- 4 + 4 + 4 = 12
- 4 $\times$ 3 = 12 
- 4 * 3 = 12  \hspace{3mm}or\hspace{3mm} 4(3) = 12 \pause

**Division**

- 12 $\div$ 3 = 4
- 12/3 = 4
- *Rational* numbers


## Exponents and roots

\LARGE

**Exponent**

- Multiply a number multiple times
- $4 \times 4 \times 4 = 64$
- $4^{3} = 64$ \pause

**Roots**

- $\sqrt[3]{64} = 4$
- $64^{\frac{1}{3}} = 4$
- *Real* numbers ($\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$)

## Negative exponents

\Huge

$$4^{-1} = \frac{1}{4}$$

$$4^{-2} = \frac{1}{16}$$

**Applies to units**

$6\:g\:l^{-1} = 6\:g/l$



## Logarithms

\LARGE

Exponent to which a number needs to be raised to get another number,

$$10^{3} = 1000.$$

10 raised to the power of 3 equals 1000,

$$\log_{10}\left(1000\right) = 3.$$



## Logarithms in figures


```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "A plot showing an exponential increase in global carbon dioxide emissions over time.", fig.cap = "Global carbon dioxide emissions 1750-2021.", out.width="100%"}
CO2_emissions   <- read.csv(file = "data/CO2_emissions.csv");
global_CO2      <- CO2_emissions[CO2_emissions[,1] == "World",];
tonnes_billion  <- global_CO2[,4] / 1000000000;
the_ylab        <- expression(Global~CO[2]~tonnes~(billions));
par(mar = c(5, 5, 1, 1));
plot(x = global_CO2[,3], y = tonnes_billion, type = "l", lwd = 4, 
     xlab = "Year", ylab = the_ylab, cex.axis = 1.25, cex.lab = 1.25);
```



## Logarithms in figures


```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "A plot showing linear increase in global carbon dioxide emissions over time, with emissions plotted on a natural log scale.", fig.cap = "Natural logarithm of global carbon dioxide emissions 1750-2021.", out.width="100%"}
CO2_emissions   <- read.csv(file = "data/CO2_emissions.csv");
global_CO2      <- CO2_emissions[CO2_emissions[,1] == "World",];
tonnes_billion  <- global_CO2[,4] / 1000000000;
the_ylab        <- expression(Log~Global~CO[2]~tonnes~(billions));
par(mar = c(5, 5, 1, 1));
plot(x = global_CO2[,3], y = log(tonnes_billion), type = "l", lwd = 4, 
     xlab = "Year", ylab = the_ylab, cex.axis = 1.25, cex.lab = 1.25);
```



## Natural logarithms

\LARGE

Exponent to which a number needs to be raised to get another number,

$$e^{1} \approx 2.718282.$$

Sometimes log denoted 'ln',

$$\ln(2.718282) = 1.$$


## Order of operations

\Huge



$$x = 3^{2} + 2\left(1 + 3\right)^2 - 6 \times 0$$



## Order of operations

\Huge

1. Brackets (parentheses)
2. Exponents \& radicals
3. Multiplication \& division
4. Addition \& subtraction



## Order of operations

\LARGE

Division bars imply parentheses,

$$x = \frac{2^{2} + 1}{3^{2} +2}.$$
\vspace{3mm}

Above is $(2^{2}+1)/(3^{2} + 2)$,

$$x = \frac{\left(2^{2} + 1\right)}{\left(3^{2} + 2\right)} = \frac{\left(4 + 1\right)}{\left(9 + 2\right)} = \frac{5}{11}.$$



## Order of operations

\Huge

Radicals imply parentheses,

$$x = \sqrt{3 + 4^{2}} = \sqrt{\left(3 + 4^{2} \right)} \approx 3.59.$$




## Tidy data: Getting data in the right format

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "Two panels are shown, the first with a woman handling a bird nest in a field setting, and the second with the same woman handling nest components over a tray in the lab.", fig.cap = "Dr Becky Boulton collects data from nest boxes in the field (A), then processes nest material in the lab (B).", out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("img/becky_field.png")
```



## Data collection is messy

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "A large tree is shown in the desert with a person dressed in tan clothes in the foreground", fig.cap = "Sonoran Desert Rock Fig in the desert of Baja, Mexico.", out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("img/Ficus_petiolaris.jpg")
```


## Data collection is messy

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "A zoomed-in portion of a lab notebook is shown, which includes handwritten data in pencil observations of mature fig fruits, including date, species, site, tree, and fruit dimensions.", fig.cap = "A portion of a lab notebook used to record measurements of fig fruits from different trees in 2010.", out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("img/handwritten_data.jpg")
```


## Observations and variables

\Huge

- **Observation:** Units of sample \vspace{2mm}
- **Variable:** Unit measurement


## Observations and variables

\Huge

- **Observation:** Fig trees  \vspace{2mm}
- **Variable:** Tree height, location

## Three characteristics of tidy data

\LARGE

Summarised by Wickham (2014)$^{1}$: \vspace{4mm}

1. Each **variable** gets its own column.
2. Each **observation** gets its own row.
3. Different units of observation require different data files.


\footnotetext[1]{Wickham, H. 2014. \textit{J. Stat. Softw.} \href{https://www.jstatsoft.org/article/view/v059i10}{59:1-23}.}

## Three characteristics of tidy data

\LARGE

| Tree  |  Species     |  Height (m)  |  Leaf length (cm)   |
|-------|--------------|--------------|---------------------|
| 1     |  Oak         |  20.3        |  8.1                | 
| 2     |  Oak         |  25.4        |  9.4                |
| 3     |  Maple       |  18.2        |  12.5               |
| 4     |  Maple       |  16.7        |  11.3               |



## File extensions and types

\Huge

- **File extensions** (e.g., PDF, JPG, DOCX)
- **Text files** (e.g., TXT, CSV, HTML)
- **Binary files** (e.g., PNG, MP3, PDF)


## File extensions and types

\Huge

- Text files can be opened by a plain text editor
- Binary files require a specific program
- Text files (e.g., CSV) are more secure long-term


## Files stored in nested folders

\Huge

- Important to organise files in a project
- One folder for a project, nested files
- Recommend a folder for SCIU4T4


## Files stored in nested folders

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "Screenshot showing a Windows directory with five folders of various modules names on the left panel.", fig.cap = "Windows file directory showing the file organisation of modules taken during spring 2006. In this case, the 'Spring\\_2006' folder is located on the desktop; the path to the folder is visible in the toolbar above the folders.", out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("img/directory_windows.png")
```
